sub_score_definitions.tco: Total Cost of Ownership: explicit annual fees (M&E + rider) plus an estimate of cap-spread drag from constrained equity exposure. Scored 100 at cohort-best drag, 0 at cohort-worst (clamped).
sub_score_definitions.gv: Guarantee Value: monetises two guarantee components. (1) Income / death rider — Monte Carlo PV of GLWB/GMDB claims divided by PV of rider fees. (2) Buffer / floor — closed-form lognormal PV of downside absorption on indexed segments divided by PV of M&E + cap-spread drag (the implicit cost base for non-rider protection). Each ratio maps 1.0x → 50 / 2.0x → 100. When both components are present, the score is a PV-weighted blend so the larger guarantee dominates. Products without ANY downside protection (no rider, no buffer / floor) default to 50.
sub_score_definitions.sf: Surrender Flexibility: rule-based deduction from 100 for surrender period years, max surrender %, low free-withdrawal corridor, and missing hardship waivers (nursing-home, terminal-illness, disability).
sub_score_definitions.ic: Insurer Credit: AM Best rating mapped to a base score, with deductions for PE ownership (-10) and high Level 3 asset concentration (-5 over 15%, -10 over 25%).
sub_score_definitions.bf: Behavioral Fairness: historical cap-rate cut frequency, illustration-vs-actual delta, NAIC complaint index, and regulatory fines in the prior 5 years. Defaults to 70 when public data is sparse.

mortality.improvement: Scale G2 projected from 2012 base year
mortality.cohort: 50/50 blended-gender qx (single scoring run, no M/F split). Mortality differential between sexes at typical annuity ages is small (≤5% of qx) and largely washes out under the PV(claims)/PV(fees) ratio that defines GV; using a blended cohort avoids gender-based scoring concerns that arise in some US states (Montana, Title VII / Norris 1983 for single-life pricing) without materially changing the composite.

allocation_profiles.rationale: Real RILA products offer multiple segments (term × index × protection level × crediting method), and the buyer's allocation across them materially shifts the contract's cost and guarantee value. Scoring a single allocation hides this dispersion. v1.4.0 scores each product three times — under a CONSERVATIVE (100% in the most-protected segment), BALANCED (equal weight across all segments), and GROWTH (100% in the least-protected segment) allocation — and reports all three composites. The published composite (and letter grade) is the BALANCED profile, preserving back-compat with v1.3.x. The conservative/growth scores are surfaced alongside so advisors can see the range of outcomes available depending on which segments the client picks.

profiles.conservative: 100% in the single segment with the highest protection level (deepest buffer or floor). Ties broken by lower cap rate.
profiles.balanced: Equal weight across all available segments. This is the published composite — same as the v1.3.x convention.
profiles.growth: 100% in the single segment with the lowest protection level (least buffer or floor).

allocation_profiles.derivation: When the product spec includes an explicit `allocation_profiles` block ({conservative,balanced,growth: [weights]}), it is used as-is. When absent, profiles are derived algorithmically from segments_available.

buffer_value_pricing.rationale: Buffer / floor absorption on indexed RILA segments is an embedded put-spread (buffer) or deep put (floor). Both have non-zero expected value under any real-world equity model with sigma > 0, so non-rider RILAs whose only guarantee is the buffer must have that value monetised — otherwise the Guarantee Value sub-score is a placeholder. Closed-form lognormal integration is used (NOT a separate Monte Carlo) for speed and byte-reproducibility; the integrand has an analytical form in terms of the standard normal CDF.
buffer_value_pricing.buffer_payoff: min(max(-R, 0), b) per segment-period, with R = exp((mu - 0.5 sigma^2) T + sigma sqrt(T) Z) - 1
buffer_value_pricing.floor_payoff: max(-R - f, 0) per segment-period; insurer absorbs all loss beyond -f
buffer_value_pricing.cost_base: PV(M&E annual rate × premium) + PV(cap-spread drag × premium), continuously discounted at scoring_scenario.discount_rate
buffer_value_pricing.mapping: score = clamp(50 × PV(absorption) / PV(cost_base), 0, 100); same shape as the rider-PV path

regimes.description: Post-Volcker disinflation through dot-com peak. High equity returns, moderate volatility.
Dot-com bust + Global Financial Crisis. Flat-to-negative cumulative returns, two major drawdowns.
Acute peak-to-trough GFC stress and early recovery. Tests buffer / floor mechanics in a single-regime drawdown.
Low-rate, low-vol bull market. Cap-constrained RILAs may underperform vs uncapped exposure in this regime.
Fed tightening, 2022 equity drawdown, 2023-24 recovery. Tests buffer mechanics in a moderate drawdown plus rapid recovery.

regime_role.rationale: Historical regimes are SUPPLEMENTARY HISTORICAL CONTEXT, not scoring inputs. The composite score depends only on the 5 sub-scores (TCO, GV, SF, IC, BF), each computed against the forward-looking standardized scoring scenario above. Per-product regime outcomes are reported alongside the rating so advisors can see how the contract would have behaved in past market environments; they do NOT enter the composite. Each regime also powers an interactive backtest scenario (see scenario_backtest below) where the user can replay any product through a chosen regime with normalized starting AV = $100.

scenario_backtest.purpose: User-facing what-if tool. Pick any product + any regime, replay the contract deterministically against actual S&P 500 monthly returns. Uses the same $250K premium as the composite scoring scenario by default — one common dollar basis across all UI surfaces (composite PV calcs and the backtest chart agree). Callers can pass an explicit starting_av to override (e.g. an advisor running with a specific client premium). Independent of scoring — does NOT affect the composite score.
scenario_backtest.output: Monthly AV path, terminal AV (in dollars), terminal AV multiple (× starting premium), max drawdown, fees-paid total.

notes: Current published methodology. FSA-signed composite rating for registered index-linked annuities (RILAs) built from 5 equal-weighted sub-scores (TCO / GV / SF / IC / BF). Scoring runs against a 50/50 blended-gender mortality cohort at age 60, $250K premium, 30-year horizon, election age 65, μ=7%, σ=18%, 4% discount, 2012 IAM (Scale G2 projection), 5,000 Monte Carlo paths, seed=42. v1.4.0 introduces MULTI-ALLOCATION scoring: each product is scored under three named profiles (conservative / balanced / growth) — see allocation_profiles. The published composite and letter grade reflect the BALANCED profile (equal weight across all segments); conservative and growth composites are reported alongside so advisors can see the full range available under different allocation choices. GV monetises two guarantee components on the same persistency basis: (1) GLWB / GMDB rider PV claims vs PV rider fees, both persistency-weighted from the Monte Carlo path; (2) buffer / floor absorption priced by closed-form lognormal integration against an M&E + cap-spread cost base, persistency-weighted and using the median Monte Carlo AV trajectory rather than a flat AV ≈ premium approximation. Cap-spread drag uses μ from this scenario (no hardcoded constants). A stress_score (worst of 5 historical regime scores) is reported alongside the composite but NOT inside it. Historical regime outcomes and the interactive backtest scenario are supplementary context, NOT composite inputs. Rating output is byte-reproducible from (product spec hash, methodology version, seed).
